note.soap:
S: 34-year-old patient. The patient is an adult female with a history of renal disease who reports dysuria described as a burning sensation when urinating. She denies pregnancy. There are no additional symptoms reported such as fever, flank pain, or vaginal discharge at this time. She has a known allergy to penicillin. The patient has not mentioned any prior treatment or current medications for this complaint.

O: Phone encounter only; unable to perform physical examination or obtain vital signs. No urine studies or other diagnostics available at this time.

A: The clinical presentation is consistent with acute cystitis, likely a urinary tract infection (UTI). Differential diagnosis includes urethritis (possibly sexually transmitted), vaginitis, or irritation from non-infectious causes. Given the patient’s renal disease and penicillin allergy, careful selection of therapy is necessary.

P: Recommend urine analysis and culture to confirm diagnosis and guide antibiotic therapy. Empiric antibiotic treatment should avoid penicillin and nephrotoxic agents. Consider prescribing a non-penicillin antibiotic with a favorable renal safety profile once culture results are available. Advise increased fluid intake and symptomatic relief measures. Acetaminophen may be used for pain or discomfort if no contraindications exist; avoid NSAIDs due to renal disease. Educate the patient to seek urgent care if she develops fever, flank pain, worsening symptoms, or signs of systemic infection. Follow-up after urine results or sooner if symptoms worsen.
note.patient_summary: Based on what you told me, your symptoms most likely suggest a urinary tract infection. Because you have a history of kidney issues and a penicillin allergy, treatment needs to be chosen carefully. Right now, increasing your fluid intake and using acetaminophen (Tylenol) can help with discomfort. A licensed physician will review this note and, if appropriate, may prescribe medication that’s safe for you. If you develop fever, worsening pain in your side or back, or if your symptoms get worse, please seek in-person care the same day. Since this is a phone visit, no tests were done today, so if you need an exam or urine testing, you would need to go to urgent care or see your primary care provider. A doctor will review everything and decide on the best next steps for you.
patient_instructions: If symptoms worsen or you develop new severe symptoms, seek urgent care or call 911.
Follow the disposition and safety net plan explained in the spoken summary.

Medication options you may consider if safe (no dosing by phone): acetaminophen (Tylenol) if you can take it safely (no dosing provided); ibuprofen (Advil) if you can take it safely (no dosing provided)
Medication cautions: Avoid NSAIDs if kidney disease, stomach ulcers/bleeding history, or on blood thinners (reviewer to confirm).
